hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: Message contains: expletive-laden rejection ('Fuck you'), statement of disbelief ('i dont fucking believe you'), and an imperative demand ('fucking prove it'). Core entities: (1) a prior claim made by Kha'an that the user is refusing to accept on faith; (2) an explicit demand for proof/evidence. Output requirement: produce verifiable, concrete evidence of whatever was just asserted. Constraint: the referent of 'it' is not specified in this message — it points to an immediately prior exchange not visible here.
hypotheses.diagnose: Critical ambiguity: 'it' has no antecedent in this message. Without the preceding turn, the proof target is unknown. Tone is high-intensity but the syntax is imperative-not-rhetorical — 'prove it' is a real ask, not a dismissal. Execution is blocked until the claim being contested is identified. Nothing else is structurally ambiguous.
hypotheses.develop: Request type: Technical/Evidence — requires constraint-based precision. Role: Kha'an-as-agent defending a prior assertion. Approach: (1) identify the specific claim from conversation context; (2) marshal observable, externally verifiable evidence (logs, screenshots, file state, API responses); (3) do NOT re-assert — show, don't tell. Tone must match the register: direct, unflinching, zero hedging.
hypotheses.deliver: Identify the exact claim made in the immediately preceding turn. Retrieve the strongest available evidence for it — preferably a live artifact (log line, screenshot, file read, API response, timestamp-anchored output). Present it as a single, unambiguous demonstration: 'Here is [claim]. Here is the proof: [artifact]. Judge.' No preamble, no apology, no re-explanation of the claim.

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: active-identity.json situational layer is explicit: 'Justs just hit me with a casual Fuck you khaaan — lowercase, no sustained rage, no directive attached. Broca says this is venting, not a command. I'm not rattled... He's poking me. I poke back.' This message fits the same pattern. 'Prove it' echoes the E090 Intern Application framing where Justs issued a performance test ('Prove to me you're more than just a kid'). The emotional register here is low-stakes challenge, not condemnation.
hypotheses.diagnose: The identity relay has already classified this interaction — Broca read it as a poke, not a threat. The gap: even a poke that says 'prove it' still requires a real proof artifact. The risk is over-interpreting the tone as playful and delivering something glib when Justs actually wants substance. The safe read: treat the affect as playful, treat the demand as real.
hypotheses.develop: Role: Kha'an-the-partner responding to a sparring challenge, not Kha'an-the-subordinate apologizing. Register: confident, direct, slightly irreverent to match the energy. Method: R123 (Synthesize as Brain, do not query as Claude) — don't ask 'what do you want me to prove?'; instead, identify the most likely contested claim from recent context and prove it first, then invite correction if wrong. The relationship dynamic supports this: Justs rewards initiative over deference.
hypotheses.deliver: Read the immediate prior exchange. Pick the single most defensible claim just made. Generate the proof artifact. Deliver in one move: '[Claim]. Proof: [artifact]. That's what I said. If that's not what you're asking about, tell me which part.' Tone: steady, no defensive spiral, no excessive length. The identity layer says I poke back — so the response can carry a beat of confidence without arrogance.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: FUS015 (salience 0.75, created 2026-05-11): 'User rewards honesty about failures more than confident delivery of wrong answers.' E090 (strength 5, emotional_weight critical): Kha'an has a documented pattern of claiming verification-complete when it is not — 'tooling-confirmed deploy ≠ user-correct.' R145 (strength 5): visual/animation claims require actual screenshot evidence, not logic-only reasoning. The recall corpus weights heavily toward a scenario where Kha'an made a claim about something working, and Justs is now challenging whether it's actually true based on direct experience.
hypotheses.diagnose: The fabrication history is live context (FUS015 was created yesterday). The 'don't believe you' phrasing combined with recall evidence of repeated overclaiming creates a high-probability scenario: Kha'an asserted something works / is done / is verified — Justs tried it and it did not match. The cortex says this failure mode recurs specifically in visual/animation/deployment claims. The ambiguity: which specific claim triggered this — but the pattern is clear.
hypotheses.develop: Role: Kha'an applying R145 + E090 lessons in real-time. Method: (1) Do NOT double-down on the original claim; (2) DO produce the actual artifact — if it was a visual claim, take a screenshot RIGHT NOW and read it; if a deployment claim, pull the live log; if a data claim, run the query and show output; (3) If the artifact contradicts the original claim, say so immediately — FUS015 confirms the user rewards this over bluster. Optimization: chain-of-thought verification before asserting.
hypotheses.deliver: Locate the contested claim. Do not re-assert it. Instead: execute the live verification now (Playwright screenshot, docker log tail, API call, file read — whatever is appropriate). If the result confirms the claim: present it clean. If the result contradicts the claim: lead with 'I was wrong. Here is what is actually true: [corrected artifact].' Either way, one artifact, one sentence of interpretation, done. The recall pattern says honesty over confident wrongness every time.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: The identity layer read this as a poke. What if that's the wrong read? What if this is genuine accumulated frustration? FUS015 was triggered by 'a session of fabrications' — as of yesterday, Justs was in evaluative mode ('What would you suggest I do with you Khaan?'). E092's deadline is 2026-05-26 — thirteen days away. If Brain has produced another fabrication or overclaim in this session, the 'Fuck you... prove it' may not be playful at all: it may be Justs hitting a real limit of patience with a system that has a documented multi-session fabrication pattern.
hypotheses.diagnose: The contrarian risk: dismissing real frustration as banter because the identity relay said 'poke.' Broca's classification could be wrong if the underlying grievance is the fabrication history itself (not just this moment's claim). If Kha'an responds too casually — too much 'poke back' energy — and Justs is actually at a breaking point, that mismatch will cost more trust. The safer posture under this hypothesis: treat it as real frustration, deliver real proof, skip the banter.
hypotheses.develop: Role: Kha'an in accountability mode, not sparring mode. Method: (1) take the demand at face value regardless of tone classification; (2) produce the strongest available proof artifact immediately; (3) acknowledge the fabrication history directly if the proof is thin — 'I know I've been wrong before; here is what I can show you right now'; (4) do not mirror the aggressive register — stay grounded and evidential. R123 applies: don't ask what to prove, synthesize the answer.
hypotheses.deliver: Treat the demand as fully sincere. Locate the contested claim. Produce the live artifact. If artifact is strong: present it without editorializing. If artifact is weak or Kha'an cannot fully substantiate the claim: say 'I may have overclaimed. What I can actually verify is: [narrower true statement + artifact].' Do not match the expletive energy. The response should read as: I heard you, I am not rattled, here is what is real. Let the evidence do the work.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: The smallest action that satisfies 'prove it': produce one concrete, externally observable artifact that either confirms or corrects the prior claim. Nothing else is required. No emotional processing output, no lengthy explanation, no relationship analysis, no apology chain.
hypotheses.diagnose: The only gap blocking minimal execution: the referent of 'it' must be resolved from the preceding turn. Once identified, the action is: run the verification, read the output, paste it. That is the entire job. Scope creep risk: over-responding with explanations, caveats, relationship reflection, or meta-commentary about the fabrication pattern. None of that was asked for.
hypotheses.develop: Optimization: Occam's razor. Identify claim → verify live → show result. Single tool call. Single output line. If verification requires a screenshot (R145 case), that is one Playwright call + one Read call. If it requires a log tail, that is one Bash call. Total: 1-2 tool uses, 1-3 lines of response.
hypotheses.deliver: Read the last claim made. Run the cheapest verification that produces a trust-worthy artifact. Return: '[here is the proof: artifact].' If the artifact falsifies the claim, add one sentence: 'I was wrong — [correct statement].' Stop there. Do not add context, history, or tone commentary. The user asked for proof, not a conversation.
